Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Fruit

The **kiwifruit**, also called simply **kiwi** or, occasionally, **Chinese gooseberry**,[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) is a fruit native to East Asia.[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Each kiwi is both the approximate size and shape of a large chicken egg,[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) being oblong and only a few inches/centimeters long.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) The thin skin is brown and fuzzy,[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) surrounding a flesh that is most commonly bright green but that can also be yellow in some varieties.[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) The translucent flesh contains many tiny black edible seeds,[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) which cluster around the whitish center.[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Kiwifruit flesh is said to taste and feel vaguely like strawberries, melon, and pineapple,[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) with a sweet tartness.[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) The fruit is firm when unripe and gradually softens as it ripens post-harvest.[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)

Like pineapple and papaya, kiwi contains enzymes that may affect the texture of dishes it's used in. Importantly, the enzymes break down connective tissue and gelatin,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7) which can be good (in the case of meat tenderizing) or bad (in the case of gelatin-set dishes).[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) Heating destroys the enzymes but also changes the flavor and texture of the kiwi itself.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:5-7)

## Seasonality

[edit | edit source]

Kiwis are commercially cultivated largely in California and New Zealand, with opposite seasons.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When selecting kiwis, look for firm fruits with no bruising or soft spots.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) Store them at room temperature until ripe,[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8) after which they should be stored in the fridge.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Before eating kiwi, the skin is typically removed. This can be done in one of two ways. In the first, the skin can be simply removed with a peeler or paring knife.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9) Alternatively, to retain the smooth and ovular shape, ripe-but-not-soft kiwis may be scooped out whole using a spoon.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-4) To do so, slice off one end of the fruit, and carefully slide the bowl of a spoon between the flesh and the skin, moving the spoon around the entire fruit to separate the skin.[[8]](#cite_note-:7-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Once this is done, you can simply pop out the whole flesh and break down as desired.[[9]](#cite_note-:8-9)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Kiwi is frequently and simply eaten raw and out of hand.[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1)[[6]](#cite_note-:2-6) It may also be used in fruit salads or as a garnish for various pastries,[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1) where the green flesh provides an extra pop of color.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) Cooking quickly causes the flesh to fall apart,[[1]](#cite_note-:6-1) which may or may not be desirable.

## Recipes
